answers.intake_q1: When did your back pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly in your back the pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any recent trauma or injury to your back or chest area?
answers.intake_q4: Any fever, chills, or unexplained weight loss since the pain started?
answers.intake_q5: Can you describe the type of pain you are experiencing (e.g., sharp, dull, burning, stabbing)?
answers.intake_q6: Any difficulty breathing, coughing, or chest pain associated with your back pain?
answers.intake_q7: Have you noticed any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your legs or feet?
answers.intake_q8: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your back pain, and if so, what were they and did they help?
answers.intake_a8: I tried tylenol but it didn't help very much
answers.intake_q9: Any history of kidney problems, recent urinary symptoms, or abdominal pain?
answers.intake_q10: Any history of cancer, osteoporosis, or recent steroid use?
evidence_search_queries: adult back pain treatment dosing acetaminophen mg duration
red flags back pain when to seek emergency care

note.soap:
S: The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of dull pain located at the left side of the lower rib cage. She denies any recent trauma, fever, chills, unexplained weight loss, difficulty breathing, coughing, chest pain, numbness, tingling, or weakness in her legs or feet. She has tried acetaminophen (Tylenol) without significant relief. There is no history of kidney problems, urinary symptoms, abdominal pain, cancer, osteoporosis, or recent steroid use. O: Phone evaluation only; no physical examination or vital signs available.

A: The working diagnosis is musculoskeletal pain localized to the left lower rib cage, likely due to a strain or minor soft tissue injury given the absence of trauma or systemic symptoms.
Differential:
1. Musculoskeletal strain or soft tissue injury
2. Costochondritis or rib inflammation
3. Intercostal nerve irritation
4. Early presentation of visceral pathology such as pleuritis or referred pain from abdominal organs

P:
- Medication safety considerations: Avoid NSAIDs if history of renal or liver disease; monitor for gastrointestinal side effects
 - Alternatives: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen if no contraindications
 - Acetaminophen 650 mg every 6 hours as needed for pain (use caution in liver disease; consider lower max daily dose)
 - Return precautions: Seek urgent care if experiencing new onset fever, worsening pain, chest pain, shortness of breath, numbness or weakness in legs, or unexplained weight loss
 - Testing: Not indicated at this time given lack of red flag symptoms; consider imaging if symptoms persist or worsen beyond 6 weeks or if new concerning features develop

evidence.title: WHO releases guidelines on chronic low back pain
evidence.snippet: The World Health Organization (WHO) is releasing its first-ever guidelines on managing chronic low back pain (LBP) in primary and community care settings, listing interventions for health workers to use and also to not use during routine care.

evidence.title: Management of Low Back Pain: Guidelines From the VA/DoD | AAFP
evidence.snippet: Low back pain is one of the most frequently experienced medical conditions, affecting 84% of adults in the United States at some point. Nearly 40% of adults will have experienced this type of pain within a three-month period. Low back pain is the leading cause of disability worldwide. The U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs and U.S. Department of Defense (VA/DoD) have published guidelines on the diagnosis and treatment of low back pain.

evidence.title: Noninvasive Treatments for Acute, Subacute, and Chronic Low Back Pain: A Clinical Practice Guideline From the American College of Physicians
evidence.snippet: In patients with chronic low back pain who have had an inadequate response to nonpharmacologic therapy, clinicians and patients should consider pharmacologic treatment with nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs as first-line therapy, or tramadol or duloxetine as second-line therapy.
